title: How to Balance Creative Passion with Parenting: Lessons from Musician Stacy Clark
guestBio: Stacy Clark is an acclaimed indie musician who has successfully transitioned into managing her children's film and television careers in Hollywood. She's also a passionate advocate for music education, founding a program dedicated to bringing music back into schools across the country.
excerpt: From indie music stages to Hollywood sets, Stacy Clark shares how she's harmonized her creative career with motherhood while championing music education. Her journey proves you don't have to choose between passion and parenting.
metaDescription: Musician Stacy Clark shares how to balance creative careers with motherhood, manage kids in entertainment, and advocate for music education.
keyTakeaways: You can maintain your creative identity while fully embracing motherhood and supporting your children's dreams.
The entertainment industry requires strong advocacy skills when managing young actors and musicians.
Music education is crucial for child development and deserves more support in our school systems.
Purpose-driven work often emerges from personal experiences and challenges you've overcome.
Balancing multiple roles requires clear boundaries and intentional time management.
Supporting your children's careers doesn't mean sacrificing your own professional identity.
Creative work can be a powerful tool for social change and community impact.

pullQuotes.text: You don't have to choose between being a creative and being a mom—you can be both beautifully.

timestamps.label: Stacy's journey from indie musician to momager
Navigating the entertainment industry with young actors
The importance of music education in schools

content:
What happens when your creative dreams collide with motherhood, and your children's talents demand their own spotlight? For acclaimed musician Stacy Clark, the answer wasn't choosing one path over another—it was learning to harmonize them all.

In this deeply personal episode of The Manage Her, Stacy sits down with host Aimee Rickabus to share her remarkable journey from indie music stages to Hollywood sets, where she now manages her children's film and television careers while maintaining her own artistic identity and championing music education in schools.

From Indie Stages to Hollywood Sets

Stacy's story challenges the traditional narrative that creative women must sacrifice their artistic pursuits when they become mothers. Instead, she's found ways to evolve her creativity, channeling her industry knowledge and passion into supporting her children's emerging careers in entertainment.

"The skills I developed as a musician—understanding the industry, networking, creative problem-solving—all of that translates beautifully into advocating for my kids," Stacy explains. Her transition from performer to "momager" wasn't about abandoning her identity; it was about expanding it.

The Reality of Managing Young Talent

Managing children in the entertainment industry comes with unique challenges that require both business acumen and fierce protective instincts. Stacy opens up about the delicate balance of nurturing her children's talents while ensuring their childhood remains intact.

She emphasizes the importance of maintaining boundaries, ensuring that her children's well-being always comes before career opportunities. "It's not about living vicariously through them," she clarifies. "It's about being their advocate and guide while they pursue their own dreams."

Championing Music Education

Perhaps most importantly, Stacy's conversation with Aimee highlights her passionate advocacy for music education in schools. Having experienced firsthand the transformative power of music, she's dedicated to ensuring future generations have access to arts education.

"Music isn't just about creating musicians," Stacy explains. "It develops critical thinking, emotional intelligence, and creativity that serves children in every area of their lives." Her music-in-schools program represents the kind of purpose-driven work that emerges when personal passion meets community need.

Integration Over Balance

One of the most powerful themes in Stacy's story is her approach to work-life integration rather than the elusive concept of "balance." She's learned to weave her various roles—musician, mother, manager, advocate—into a cohesive life that honors all aspects of her identity.

This integration requires intentional choices and clear priorities. Stacy shares practical strategies for managing multiple responsibilities while staying connected to her creative core and maintaining her marriage and family relationships.

The Power of Purpose-Driven Creativity

What sets Stacy apart is her commitment to using her platform and talents for greater impact. Whether she's performing, managing her children's careers, or advocating for music education, she approaches each role with the same level of intentionality and purpose.

"Creativity without purpose feels empty to me now," she reflects. "I want everything I do to contribute to something larger than myself."

Lessons for Creative Parents

Stacy's journey offers valuable insights for any parent trying to maintain their professional identity while supporting their children's dreams:

  • Your skills are transferable: The abilities you've developed in your career can serve your children in unexpected ways.
  • Evolution isn't abandonment: Changing how you express your talents doesn't mean giving up who you are.
  • Advocacy is a skill: Whether you're advocating for your art, your children, or your cause, strong advocacy skills serve you everywhere.
  • Purpose amplifies everything: When your work serves a larger mission, it becomes more fulfilling and impactful.

The Harmony Continues

As Stacy continues to navigate her multifaceted career, she remains committed to showing other women that they don't have to choose between their various callings. Her story is a testament to the power of creative adaptation and the importance of staying true to your values while embracing new roles.

For listeners juggling their own complex lives, Stacy's message is clear: you can honor all parts of yourself. It takes intentionality, creativity, and sometimes hard choices, but the result is a life that feels authentic and full.

Whether you're a creative professional, a parent with big dreams, or someone looking to make a difference in your community, Stacy Clark's story offers both inspiration and practical wisdom for harmonizing the different notes of your life into a beautiful, purposeful symphony.

quiz.title: What's Your Creative-Life Balance Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally navigate the intersection of creativity, career, and personal life. Take this quiz inspired by musician Stacy Clark's journey of harmonizing artistic passion with motherhood.

questions.question: When your life circumstances change dramatically, how do you typically handle your creative pursuits?

options.text: I find new ways to express my creativity that fit my current situation
I focus on supporting others' creative dreams and put mine on the back burner
I look for ways to align my creativity with a bigger purpose or cause
I create structured time blocks to ensure my creativity doesn't get lost

questions.question: What motivates you most when you're working on a creative project?

options.text: The excitement of trying something new or experimenting with different approaches
Knowing that my work will somehow benefit or inspire the people I care about
The potential to make a meaningful impact on my community or industry
The satisfaction of completing something well within the time I've allocated for it

questions.question: When someone you love has a big opportunity that requires your support, you:

options.text: Get excited about learning new skills and adapting to help them succeed
Immediately start researching everything you need to know to protect their interests
Think about how this opportunity could also serve a larger purpose or help others
Carefully plan how to support them while maintaining your own commitments

questions.question: How do you view the relationship between your various roles and identities?

options.text: They're all part of one evolving story—each role informs and enriches the others
Some roles are more important than others, and I'm willing to sacrifice for what matters most
They all serve my deeper mission—different expressions of the same core values
They each deserve dedicated time and attention, and I work to honor them all

questions.question: What's your biggest concern when juggling creative work with other major life responsibilities?

options.text: That I might get stuck in old patterns instead of adapting to new realities
That the people I'm supporting might not get everything they need from me
That I might lose sight of the bigger picture and why my work really matters
That I might not be giving each area of my life the focused attention it deserves

questions.question: When you imagine your ideal creative legacy, you see:

options.text: A body of work that shows growth and reinvention across different life phases
The success and happiness of the people you've championed and supported
Meaningful change in your field or community that outlasts your individual contributions
A well-lived life where you honored both your creativity and your other commitments

results.title: The Creative Evoluter
results.description: You have a gift for reinventing your creative expression as your life changes. Rather than seeing transitions as obstacles, you view them as opportunities to expand your artistic identity. You're skilled at finding new ways to channel your talents, even when circumstances shift dramatically. This episode will resonate deeply with you as Stacy shares how she transformed her music industry experience into advocating for her children while maintaining her own creative voice.

results.title: The Fierce Protector
results.description: You lead with your heart and your protective instincts are your superpower. When it comes to the people you care about, you're willing to learn any skill and fight any battle to support their dreams. You naturally put others' needs first, but sometimes struggle to maintain your own identity in the process. Stacy's insights about being a 'momager' while preserving boundaries will give you practical strategies for protecting both your loved ones and yourself.

results.title: The Purpose Pioneer
results.description: You're driven by a deep sense of mission and believe in using your talents to create meaningful change. Your creative work isn't just about self-expression—it's about impact. You see connections between personal experiences and larger social issues, and you're not afraid to advocate for what matters. Stacy's passion for music education and social change through creativity will inspire you to think bigger about your own purpose-driven work.

results.title: The Harmony Seeker
results.description: You excel at creating systems and boundaries that allow all parts of your life to coexist peacefully. You understand that balance isn't about perfect equality—it's about intentional choices and clear priorities. You're naturally good at time management and finding creative solutions when different roles seem to conflict. This episode will validate your approach and give you even more tools for maintaining the harmony you've worked so hard to create.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Welcome back to The Manager, where we explore inspiring journeys of women navigating life, work, and family with strength and grace. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager: Unveiling the Invisible Work, and Sparking Leadership Revolution. Now available on Amazon.

Today's episode is a special one. I'm joined by Stacy Clark, an acclaimed singer songwriter whose music has been featured on One Tree Hill, The Hills, and Keeping Up with the Kardashians. But beyond the spotlight, Stacy is also a devoted mom of two, a passionate mama, and a woman who wears many hats. Wife, artist, advocate, and now family through a special connection we share. My sister-in-law, married to my brother, is her husband's sister, so we bonded through a beautiful family thread.

Throughout this conversation, we'll explore how Stacy balances creativity and caregiving, business and intuition, all while navigating the often invisible labor so many women shoulder. And stay with us until the end of the episode. You'll hear two of Stacy's newest songs, Little Out of Focus, which is our theme song, and Sugar in the Air. They're beautifully introspective, airy, and emotional songs that speak to the journey of self-reflection, resilience, and joy. So, let's dive in.

Stacy, after reading chapter one of The Manager, which explores invisible labor and the call to rise up, how would you describe a day in the life of a mom, mamaager, wife, and singer, songwriter?

**Stacy:** Complete chaos. I think it's a full life. It's a beautiful life. And I think every person, you have times where you're just like, "Okay, I'm just gonna get up and do my best." Then that's all you can do, you know?

**Aimee:** Like in a normal day, what would you say? Do you have a routine?

**Stacy:** Yeah. I usually get up around 7:00 a.m. I make sure the kids lunches are made. I feed them breakfast, get them out the door, bring them to school. Honestly, I'm very OCD. I like to have everything clean before I can get anything done. So once a week I wash the car, I clean it out or I go grocery shopping, I fix all the food and stuff because I really try to keep us eating healthy, especially just has been like a brewer for being sick.

And then once I have everything I feel organized, then I can either focus on work I have to do, emails, the kids if they have auditions, preparing scripts, really just setting that all up, anything music related, non-music related, and then hopefully finding some time to exercise. That's something I'm really prioritizing. My husband and I like going on walks and if I can't do that I try to go hiking with a girlfriend. And then before you know it it's time to pick up the kids and then bring them to all their activities and then that's full-on with the homework.

They usually have auditions like last night I was up to midnight editing an audition for my son and then put the kids to bed but then I'm still up so I try to prioritize sleep as well but there are some days when there's deadlines or things I need to get on. That's where it gets a little tricky.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. Totally. Yeah. So, always trying to find the time to squeeze everything in. And because we're such perfectionists and we things done a certain way, it's keeping all the things, you know, making sure the kids eat well and keeping it to the level of our personal standard while we get all of the things done. It's lots.

**Stacy:** Yeah. The visible and invisible labor that goes into running the house. I feel for me I had today I was like we just got back from Tahoe and we still have despite doing so much laundry there's still laundry and skis and snowboards everywhere and I just had to be like it's okay, it's okay I don't have to put everything away you know but I just feel maybe it's just something I create for myself or just knowing that I'm the only one who's going to do it or it won't get done. I just feel pressure to always get stuff done.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah. I think we always feel under pressure. I think moms have this neverending to-do list, you know? I never experienced time anxiety until I became a mom. And I've heard of these things and I'm always like, but it's a real thing. And I hate being late. I was always late growing up. I was always the last kid picked up. And my mom did her best. You know, she was a single mom. But that to me, I'm just like we have to go. We have to go.

And my kids are just very almost European. During the week they will wake up right you know 10 minutes 15 minutes before going to school but if it's the weekend they're up at 6:00 a.m. And I'm like why? And they're like because we have the whole day and it drives me crazy but this is just one of the fun things about parenting you know you could sleep in and they are not going to but during the week when you have to get up you have to wake them from the dead and it's just this fun little game we play.

**Stacy:** We do the exact same thing. I experienced that this morning. I was like, "Oh, good." I'm like, "I will have an extra hour this morning. I will get up an hour early before the kids get up." Nope. They were up the one day they don't have anywhere to go today. Angie's on spring break. Nobody has anywhere to go, but nope. 7 a.m. Let's go, Mom.

**Aimee:** I know. It's this fun little game. It's a fun little game we play.

So, as a mom, wife, and full-time creative, how do you balance your personal life and your music career?

**Stacy:** I feel that's just something that's always in progress. I really try to make time for friends, but I'm going be honest. A lot of times we're just so busy with I see all my music friends. I see all the people adults that my kids say if they play sports, I'll see all those parents acting. I'll make new friends on set. But it's really those important relationships. You have to make time for them. Even if it's a couple times a year, once a month, you have to put effort into it because it's really challenging.

And the more things you want to get done, whether it's I want to prioritize recording, so I'll be recording with Corey or writing. We have a songwriting project as well. And it's really important to me to always create. So, it's just carving out time, setting boundaries on your time, and just being realistic with what you can commit to. Because I used to be a real yes person and now I'm just you can't because you'll burn out.

And also I enjoy taking on a lot of volunteer positions. I brought a music program to the kids school the past two years. I brought a free acting program. I'm really try to bring opportunities for children, you know, because I think it's so important the music and all those things. But sometimes when it's just such a battle to get these programs and I'm it's worth fighting for. But sometimes you have to ask yourself, okay, I'm doing as much as I can, but you have to be realistic about your time and boundaries.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. I think time management is the key to success in life, but especially in motherhood.

**Stacy:** Yeah. It's I'm actually been thinking about how can I use AI to create almost an algorithm for my life to break my time down so that I make enough time for my husband, my kids, myself, my career, you know, my hobbies, writing. I'm working on another book. When do I have time? I usually do it when I'm driving.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And honestly, I write songs when I'm driving. A lot of times we're in LA bringing the kids for auditions or callbacks or filming. I will have song ideas. I'll make notes in my phone or just carving out that time where you're I need this for me, you know, to be happy. And it's so important because I feel if you don't do that, then you can almost be resentful or just not as happy. I notice if I don't walk as much, or exercise, I'm just not as happy and I don't I want to be the best version of myself, you know? I think that's so important.

**Stacy:** Yeah. I tell my kids it's kind of we have these human bodies and they're kind of our pets and we have to make sure they get we feed them the right food. They get enough to drink, they get enough sleep, and they get enough exercise. It's if we had a pet, we would take better care of it than we take our care of ourselves sometimes.

**Aimee:** It's true. Especially even the water thing. I mean, that's such a real thing. It's you would think our bodies are made of water. We need water, but it's just all those little things so important.

**Stacy:** Yeah. I think that that's one of those big things you got to instill in your kids is how do we take care of our bodies in that special way, too?

**Aimee:** Oh, especially even with vitamins. I find if my son isn't getting vitamins B's he's just more moody and folate and so I'm just did you take your vitamins? We haven't had them in a couple days. Okay, I see it.

**Stacy:** If Julian hasn't had his multivitamin, it is so obvious. He's so much more moody.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And I feel even for me, I'm just "Okay, did I am I methylating? Did I get that B?" I know it sounds crazy, but these are things I know I have the MTHFR gene. I know I don't methylate. I know I need these things to be, you know.

**Stacy:** Me too. I know. It's amazing how we've become so much more aware in the information age. We do have a deeper understanding of our bodies than we ever had before.

**Aimee:** Oh, I feel when I was younger, I was so hyperactive, but when I look back, I'm I had Flintstone vitamins, if that, you know what I mean? And I'm just, you know, and it wasn't until college that I realized so much about food and the glossilite and all that stuff and just changing then it's oh, I went from barely getting a C in school to being on the honor dean's list. But I feel nutrition is such a huge part of life and it's how would you know? You know what I mean?

**Stacy:** Yeah, it really is. Well, I think we've come a long way with nutrition in the last, you know, 13 years or so with the understanding epigenetics and how nutrigenomics and how different bodies need different foods and different vitamins and minerals. It's pretty cool. I love that part of things, too. That's amazing that changing your diet was able to change your performance totally in your academics.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. And gluten. and I realized I have a gluten sensitivity that causes brain fog and honestly canker sores and rashes and I thought I had celiac but it's just no I just react. So it's really interesting and so important to be in tune with these things. I mean do I occasionally eat gluten? Who doesn't? But do you know what I mean? It's just being aware to optimize your brain and your energy.

**Stacy:** Yeah, totally. I love that. And okay, a little different subject, but how has becoming a mother changed your songwriting?

**Aimee:** Oh, it's completely changed my songwriting in that I just feel there's no pressure. I just don't care what people think. I'm very happy. I want to be a good representative for my kids to see. You can do whatever you want. You can write whatever you want and be you. It's that Dr. Seuss saying, you know, what is it? The people who care or something it's a really good saying. I'm botching it, but people who mind or don't mind. You have to Google it and say it insert here Dr. Seuss saying, but it's just the people who matter don't mind and the people who mind don't matter.

And that's such a good So, it's just be yourself and be authentically you. And I feel having kids and being a songwriter, I'm being more authentic and creating things that are, oh, I want to create this positive world. I want to bring people into the French Riviera, so or this happy time in my life, you know? So just opening up those chapters rather than I feel pre-kids and pre-marriage, you know, you have a lot breakups and which I still will write about friendship breakups or this or that or you know if you're angry at your family, you know, you can put it in a song, but I feel yeah, it's just a happier, more joyful time in my life and I just try to reflect that.

**Stacy:** I love that. Tell me about your journey as a singer songwriter how does this happen? How when do you start writing music?

**Aimee:** I think it's really funny. My life, it's a it's a comedy in a way. But also a tragedy comedy, but I feel so long story short, when I was eight, my dad got in a car accident and we lost him and my mom took me to a therapist and so they taught me you just have to write about how you feel and always just let those emotions out. Don't hold them in. And so I just always did that. And my grandmother's sister, who is actually a Catholic nun, got me a strawberry shortcake notebook. And I would just write my feelings all the time.

And then I wanted to learn an instrument. My first instrument was a clarinet, but you clearly can't perform. So I taught myself the guitar. Which was challenging. I mean, I literally worked jobs, saved money as a soccer referee. And then I went and I bought my guitar, taught myself, and my mom was "Who's is that?" I'm "It's mine. I'm going to do this." And I just always have been really driven, knowing what I want to do.

And then from there, I just I just loved music. I also played soccer, but when I was in college, before college and high school, I got really sick. I had an immunity disorder, which I'm pretty sure is through the tetanus shot. I was behind on a lot of vaccines for college. And so my doctor gave me literally five at once. So I was completely healthy. And then then I went from being healthy to hemophilic only 30 something crazy 15,000 platelets and I was basically a girl in a bubble. So then I had to get a blood plate transfusion and all of these things go on prednisone steroids which was just wild.

So, I didn't, you know, I got to play soccer D3, but my life was just completely different. You know, I wasn't really sure. I've always wanted to do music, but I had to go to college. My mom was, you know, you have to always be able to support yourself. This is the way or you're not in the family. And I'm really glad she did that because I had such a great experience in college and I met so many great people.

But it was really I knew I wanted to do music and having my life shift, you know, from wanting to do soccer and stuff, I just was, okay, it's clear this is my path. And then I just knew I had to fight for it. And I did. And there's no, hey, this is how you become a musician. I was living in Long Island and I'd go to New York a ton and lug my guitar around and perform and audition for things.

And then I just was my first tour I did when I was 19. I toured all over the US and I booked it around all my friends I met in college. So I started at SUNY Plattsburgh in upstate New York but I also studied at University of Hawaii and I always find the loopholes. You can go to the Hawaii exchange not going in state and paying instate prices. And so I got all my friends and we all went there and it was the best time of our lives.

But then I felt I had to come back to reality because I'm and I graduated I started at Buffalo State then Hawaii then Plattsburgh. I made friends all over book tours and I feel having friends is so important and I just learned so much from those experiences and then I would just grind, you know, write, record. My first internship was when I was 15 at a high school. I fought for the program. They were going to cut it from our high school. I had to perform and sing. So, I've always been advocating for the arts, fighting for the arts.

**Stacy:** Yeah. And making music. So, how old were you when you wrote your did your first album?

**Aimee:** 14, holy moly. And then 15 I recorded it. And I worked many jobs to fund it. I did it all on my own. And it's just why? My first album was actually culture. It's not available if you have it. But it was just really innocent the songwriting and it's just depicts a time in high school where you're just the confusion what is my purpose?

And it's just we I would go to festivals, Christian festivals, and then sing camp sites and sell my CD. And I really feel Girl Scouts selling cookies taught me how to, you know, sell music and all these wonderful little things in life that make you who you are.

**Stacy:** I love that. That's amazing. So you really leveraged your community around you which we also is part of our intellectual capital in my book that would be called considered intellectual capital. It's leveraging the people around you. So you were able to do that to put together your first tour at 19. That's really smart.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. That's amazing. I love that.

**Stacy:** So there's so much invisible labor that goes into both the creative and the caretaking. What's something that people don't often realize about you? the emotional or mental load that you carry.

**Aimee:** I feel maybe people don't realize I do as much as I do. Because I take on a lot of roles, but I also feel because I'm in a position where I can do more, I am called to do more. Community service is so important and then also just doing as much as I can for my kids and their friends. I manage a soccer team which is full-on because I also book the scrimmages and we do a lot of fundraising because I want to make sure everybody can afford to do all of these you know wonderful things and I think it's yeah just volunteering and being present absolutely but then yeah just trying to do music and carve out time for that and then carve out time to be you said be a wife and be a friend and that's yeah just a lot.

Yeah. The sometimes the invisible labor is just being the manager of how do we take how do we manage this this small amount of time that we get together and that's the thing our families both live not here not in California. So just when we see our families, really being present and enjoying that time as well, which you know, I'm from Buffalo, New York, so we spend a lot of time there. And then we also do a lot of time in Phoenix, Arizona. And I just Yeah. I just feel that's so important, too, because especially as our parents are growing older and knowing they're not going to be here forever, it's so important to prioritize that time. And that's something that's so important.

**Stacy:** You guys do a really good job of that. You really do. how long do you guys spend in New York?

**Aimee:** We do three weeks every year. I love that. And then Arizona will go a couple weeks a year. I mean, it's not a bad drive. And we're just our kids, if we did anything right, it's they're road warriors, so you know. They're just used to being in the car. And we go to Tahoe, we go to Arizona, we just put them in the car and go. And it's really great, you know.

**Stacy:** Yeah. It's so good. We just did a road trip with I did a road trip with the three older boys. We went to Arizona a week ago and it was so fun. It's been a while since I just threw the kids in the car. And I think everybody missed it. I think they all had such a good time just being together.

**Aimee:** Road trips are so fun, you know. And Yeah. I love it. Yeah. Just go and visit the grandparents and visit Uncle Paul and go see Auntie and everybody. I think it was such a good time for them. They had a really good time.

**Stacy:** It is. And it's special because you're on vacation, but you're also seeing family. So, there's definitely perks, but obviously I would love our family to live down the street, you know?

**Aimee:** Oh, I know. And me, too. I mean, I'd love to drop the kids off. I always feel I don't want to say jealous. I just I feel people who have grandparents who live near them or their parents who they can just they're what a gift. It's a whole different thing.

**Stacy:** I know. I've watched this guy on Instagram and he says that this generation that we're in, being parents right now, this is the hardest it's been on parents since World War II because of the way that our parents aren't around to help us with our kids. We don't have the community, the support systems that we have had before. And it's you can't fault people because it's really hard to retire in California. And you know the weather when you get older you get colder and Arizona is really you know great and Florida for retiring and yeah my whole family's still in Buffalo so it's just I'm the black sheep.

**Aimee:** I know but yeah it's hard to not have your family around you when you're raising you know when you're raising kids. But the on the other side we have friends that have become family and I'm so grateful for that. You know that's so that's the key and I think that's part of the community and what what what were the work that we're doing with the managers bringing awareness to the fact that we might not have the grandparents we used to but maybe we should be building some other type of support system and community to help one another.

**Stacy:** So huge. So huge. Yeah. It's how can we as women be more intentional in creating community for ourselves so that we have the support that we need. That's why I did the whole flower child festival to bring women together. And just have accessibility to non-toxic products. Also breastfeeding, normalizing that and also screening for learning and speech and all the things that nobody prepares you for, you know, and it's just so important to have that community, normalize everything, and just be there for each other.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah. We as women, I think we we are coming back together. Yes. Yeah. And I think as we do, it's going to get easier again. We're just We went through a little bit of a weird time. I think social media kind of can divide, but I think if it's we use it intentionally, there's a way for us to create community for real.

**Stacy:** I agree. Yeah. It's setting that intention of doing something real instead of something that's just surface level or Yeah.

So, do you have any systems songwriting rituals or boundaries around your time that help you stay productive while managing your home life?

**Aimee:** Yes. So, I feel my friend Corey, who you heard before perform, just he has a recording studio and he lives down the street and I feel just having safe space where you can go and write and bounce ideas off each other and just it's so nice, you know? So, I still write songs at home and everything, but just I feel having a place not at home to really focus on your craft is just so important. So then you're not distracted from your work. And I think it's great to be able to work remote, but it's also good to have that. And it's been a huge difference in my writing and, you know, my time management.

Yeah. I mean, every day my husband and I will go on walks and we'll try to prioritize that. Because sometimes it's house divided. You're bringing one kid somewhere, the other, you know, he's bringing the other kid somewhere else. and just trying to find time whether it's on the weekend with all the family and then during the week with each other. That's so important.
